author: @ClownWhisper
text: Years ago I made a swamp cooler similarly but it wasn't anything to look at. I lived in a house that basement was dug two block 2D into the water table so my sump pump ran almost constantly at least once every two minutes it would rob and the water was ice cold so instead of pumping it directly outside I pumped it through two radiators and in between the two radiators I put a standard fan I ran that all out through the basement window up into a window out side they went let into one of my bedrooms and if I kept the door shut it got my bedroom down to almost 65 degrees! I figured that water was being run all day and night anyway that some pump was costing me a fortune to run so I might as well utilize. At one time I wanted to make a big sistern that would once full release its water through some sort of generator. I never thought to build the generator I was looking for something commercial that would run on the water pressure which was available but I never ended up doing it

author: @TSmith4305
text: You could use a twin below to double the output so it is blowing on both strokes and a simple pulley system to reduce the strain on the motor to make it possible. Combine that with heat plates to draw the heat from the heat exchange and it would significantly increase its cooling. A thermionic generator would work as well and also give back some of the electricity used to power the device. Just an idea. You inspire me and your energy and joy in the inventive process is exhilarating!

author: @DonGeatschos
text: so if there is 0.04% co2 in the air that would mean you need around 140 strokes to move 1 liter of co2. At 0.178 grams per liter co2 and if you swap the radiator for something longer lasting than soda lime (maybe a liquid filter), you would need around 780.000 strokes to filter out 1kg of co2. At around 2 seconds per stroke this would take around 18 days at the cost of 0.216 kWh. At 200grams co2 per kilometer driven (average petrol engine) you would make up for a 5 km car drive to work at the cost of 0.216 kWh and 18 days of time (i did not factor in the cost of the soda lime if there is no better substitute, that would also be around 50€ for an absorbtion of 1kg co2 (i did not look up the current prices)). i was rather more optimistic at the start of this comment when i still had not done the calculations ... i actually thought that would be a good idea to make :D ... but great video series, thank you !!!

author: @William_Hada
text: Initially, I was surprised that the bellows were that much more efficient than a fan. But thinking more about it the bellows has a much more direct effect on air movement because the volume of air is confined inside the bellows with no air leakage possible. The fan, an air screw, has a lot of leakage and can interact with and be effected by the surrounding air in the room. Lots of interference and losses. Anyway, great device. I love it!

author: @boots7859
text:
Don't want to be pedantic, however this isn't an air conditioner.  Its a mechanical bellows.
It is simply an alternative air induction device alternative to a fan.
Now, having one of those in your living room, dining room or bedroom might initially be seem neat, however in most households that sort of noise pollution just isn't going to cut it with SWMBO'd.
10W * 24 * 30 = 8 Kwh/month. So maybe a $1 or more for almost silent, more airflow with a fan.
Neat, however don't see much practicality for it.

replies.author: @MrTubeuser12
replies.text: do you actually pay attention ?  by passing hot/cold water through the radiator then passing air over the radiator you are technically "CONDITIONING" the air by modifying it's temperature, therefore the bellows, by virtue of it's action, is the air "CONDITIONER" . on top of that you completely missed the whole ethos of the video !  so yes you are been pedantic !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: most fans are considered quiet at 32dB -  a m sure this is lower - and remember we are actually using the radiator to do something - so no - not just a bellows mate

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text:
I'm guessing you're unaware of the videos leading up to this build.  The bellows are simply the method used for moving air through the heat exchanger (radiator) located on the front.  The unit as a whole is the "air conditioner" - conditioning the air to a desired temperature.
The purpose of this isn't to replace all modern air-conditioners, but is a cheap and easy-to-build alternative that can be made with scavenged parts.  Frequently, Westerners look at solutions with a "consumption" mindset.  "Why build that, when I can just go out and buy a new one from Walmart?"  Ever since I spent a time homeless with almost no funds, I've learned to look at things MUCH differently.  I now ask, "why BUY that, when I can cheaply build one myself?"   Cheap, sustainable living is big on my mind.
In that sense, this is far more "practical" for my needs.  I can "recycle" or "upcycle" parts to create this for easily less than $50 (if not free!), and this would be easy to run from off-grid power.  10 watts per hour doesn't sound like much, but adds up quickly when you're only generating and living off of 3000 watts per day.  10watts/hour could come out to nearly 10% of daily consumption.  This is also more versatile, allowing me to use it for both heating and cooling, and it can be easily integrated with other systems.
Size is really the major trade-off with this solution.  The bellows take up perhaps 20+ times as much space as an equivalent fan.  That's definitely a fair criticism as regards "practicality", but may not be an issue at all depending on your application and placement.
I also wouldn't focus too strongly on the "noise" of this specific build.  Rob used easy, cheap and readily available methods for both the bellows and the motor.  While it's already as quiet or quieter than a fan would be, it wouldn't be difficult to design this in a way to be "whisper quiet" where you'd only hear a light "whoosh" of air moving at a few decibels.
Overall, I think what Rob intends to show us with videos like this one are methods and ideas that we can build on and apply to our individual needs.  It's not meant as a replacement or "best way" to do things.
By the way... I say all the above as the SWMBO you mentioned.  😋  This would "cut it" with me just fine.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
Sounds like an iron lung! Ha-ha!   Thought about something more conventional, with evaporating alcohol in a sealed radiator or something?

"""I'm just curious, does this thing scale up?,,,,,

I guess this would work well for those living in mountain areas, or with springs, or Wells, a gravity spring with 45° water. piped in to something the like , would work well. And just put a coil of copper pipe on the wood stove, for heat , if the plumbing is clean and lead free, the water could be used in the house, I have a shallow well, and was thinking about pumping the cold water into a insulated tank, (like a water heater tank) using a small 12/24v pump, being I'm working on a radiator heating system, and don't need heavy air conditioning, this could work well!. My house is in the shade mostly,  the end of June to the end of August is still a bit warm mid day!   I was thinking about using a aftermarket car radiator fan, with a pwm controller, to slow it down, something 10"-12' although 16" or possibly larger is an option. They can move near 3000cfm, but I'm not building a wind tunnel! Ha-ha, my thinking a single fan, on pwm, spinning slowly, would be more efficient than 6-8 120mm computer fans, not to mention cheaper, I have a couple 12" I gave like $10 for used, but eBay has then for about $25-$30 for two,  nice job!

author: @TheKlickitat
text: Robert, why have you stopped with the batteries? It seems from an uninformed outside observer that you are pumping out tons of videos to make Youtube money. I love your videos and am a big fan. I think however that if your battery ideas work as well as you claim, there is a huge market for custom one-offs or short runs that could keep a person like yourself in full-time employment making really good money. I work in the UAV industry doing similar things but with mounting and payload integration. I know for a fact that an entity that could provide high energy density on a custom scale would be in high demand in the UAV industry. Let me know how to get a hold of you and we can chat about it some if you are interested. You won't make your millions on a quick deal but if you deliver what you talk about, you could easily make six figures.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text:
"tons of videos to make Youtube money..." "Robert Murray-Smith,162K subscribers" lol i dont think so mate...
did you actually do your part, and at least advocate new people sub to the channel if  not support him directly as a member etc in your comments to get him to at least 1 million+ subs

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we have very different experiences mate

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: I think it would be a more polite (and more successful) approach to simply ask if he could do more battery content, that you really enjoy it and/or it helps you in your industry.

replies.author: @TheKlickitat
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I guess I tried to put too many things in a small space. First I love your channel. I  watch almost everything you do.  I seem to be drawn to your stuff because I feel that maybe we are kindred spirits. I always find what you do interesting.  Will always be a fan of this channel no matter what.  It also seems that I combined a nosey question with an assumption with some encouragement and it came out all wrong.  I was wondering out loud as to why you moved away from the battery/capacitor realm of things and into general energy topics. I love all of them btw.  In the end, I was trying to give you an idea of a direction to take your battery development.  I love what you do and will always follow your creative mind.  Cheers and may God bless you in your endeavors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @TheKlickitat No worries mate - I haven't moved away from batteries I just don't make that many videos on it at the moment but it is still my main focus and something I work on all the time - but I am also aware that lots of people just aren't interested in batteries and there are other energy issues that do interest them and may help - so I explore those too. Given there is just me and only so much time in a day I do what I can and what feels important to me as that is my main gauge - I hope this helps, rob

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
It's fantastic. My wife would kill me if I drag that into the house as our new ac unit. Still it's fantastic.

The fan vs what you build efficiency is somewhat discutable. Motors hardly ever consume as much power as rated for. I think you compared based on rated power so your calculation might be not very correct.

But it's great. I could watch it for hours  .....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - glad you liked it mate and I don't want to be a pedant here - most of the time you are right - but that's because motor power consumption depends on load and as load varies so does the power consumed - I believe air fans are mostly working on constant load - shifting a known volume of air at sea level pressure - ergo power consumption is going to be fairly even

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Maybe so. I only measured several 12V DC fans once and they didn't consume even half. I think power consumption is used for fuse and driver circuit calculation and obviously when these fans are ramping up they consume the most power, maybe long enough to blow a slow fuse or kill a driver circuit. I guess as long as you're not commercializing ( Amusement parks might want it ) it doesn't matter much :-).

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Imagine a few fan generators from computer fans that you have previously covered placed in the pipe that feeds the cool air from another place (cellar etc) that fed into a capacitor bank or battery (or both) to run the motor. You could then run this entirely free. You could even also add in a very small solar panel. Even a fan on the outfeed could add to the power conversion.
I love the progression of this project and how it has evolved using the community ideas of your viewers. Agree you may have taken it a step or three overboard but that's where a lot of the fun comes into play, right?
I eagerly await other projects completed using your community.

author: @btryba
text:
Ok this is pretty awesome! I work in the HVAC industry in the US, so I needed to do the unit conversions to grasp exactly what you've done.

The purchased fan is producing 350 - 600 cubic feet per watt at maybe 1/4 inch WG of static pressure if you're lucky, but your bellows fan is producing 1900 cubic feet and the static pressure has to be rather decent. The CFM is 15.8 which like you said isn't a lot of air, but there is lot of potential with the pressures you can achieve and the raw efficiency of cubic foot per watt is awesome.

replies.author: @gominosensei2008
replies.text: Static pressure should be the weight of the bellows divided by the area of the crossection minus duct and radiator losses, and could be altered by placing weights on top of it, as long as it is still something the motor can pull up....

author: @chriswood1581
text: I wonder what effect a double acting bellows system would have on the efficiency. It feels like you could get the motor doing usable work through its full cycle then rather than idling for half a turn.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
Not a bad idea. It would still be cool to have 3-bellows system so you're always pumping a constant flow of air, with no pulsing. You could probably also put a counterweight on the motor's output so that when the bellows is on its way down you're storing that energy and vice versa.

I'm really impressed with the efficiency of the thing already though, and more moving parts might add too much friction. Also, the specs from that silent fan are probably with no restriction, so when you push he output through the radiator it'd make it significantly worse than the spec. I think this benefits from being so big it can't help but be efficient.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Just like full wave rectification rather than half wave. Great idea!

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername very true, but adding a few fan generators from computer fans inside the intake pipe where you draw the cool air from would counter the power losses caused from friction. Add a small solar panel and it costs next to nothing to run.

replies.author: @jamesbrown99991
replies.text: Adding a counterweight to the drive-wheel should be able to increase efficiency, without needing an extra bellows. Potential energy is stored in the weight during the return stroke, and assists with the pull stroke.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sure it would mate
